title: Flat Rate for Freelancers 2025 — Requirements and Pitfalls
description:
Are you going to register as a freelancer? The flat rate allows you to pay just $80 per month for the first year. I explain all the requirements, duration, bonuses, and pitfalls that no one tells you. Updated US/UK tax law 2025.

Personal finance, flat rate freelancers, freelancer fees, freelancer registration, tax authority.

segments.voice: If you're thinking of becoming a freelancer, there's a bonus that can save you over $3,000 in the first two years. It's called the flat rate and allows you to pay just $80 per month in self-employment tax during the first twelve months. But there are pitfalls that no one tells you, and today I'll explain them all.
segments.visual: person starting new business laptop excited

segments.voice: The flat rate is regulated according to tax law, which established a new system of taxation based on real income. This bonus applies from January 1, 2023, with the new conditions of the income tax system.
segments.visual: tax law documents official gazette

segments.voice: The requirements to access the flat rate are as follows. First, you must not have been registered as a freelancer in the previous two years, or three years if you previously enjoyed the flat rate. Second, you must not be a family collaborator. And third, you must not have outstanding debts with the tax authority or the social security system.
segments.visual: checklist requirements document approval stamp

segments.voice: During the first twelve months, you pay a reduced fee of $80 per month, regardless of your income. This includes taxation for common contingencies, professional contingencies, and cessation of activity. It's a fixed fee that doesn't vary with the income tax bracket you declare. That's $960 per year, compared to over $3,000 without the bonus.
segments.visual: calculator savings money comparison dollars coins

segments.voice: But here's the first pitfall. When those twelve months are over, your fee is calculated based on your real income from the tax system. If during the first year you declared net income above the minimum wage, the increase can be brutal. You can go from $80 to $300 or $400 per month overnight. Many new freelancers don't plan for this jump.
segments.visual: graph rising costs shock surprise person

segments.voice: There is an extension of the flat rate. If, after the first twelve months, your net income is below the minimum wage, which in 2025 is $1,134 per month, you can request an extension for another twelve months at $80 per month. That's a total of twenty-four months at a reduced fee. This is according to tax law.
segments.visual: calendar extension time twelve months bonus

segments.voice: Second important pitfall. The flat rate applies to the minimum taxation base. That means that during the time you enjoy it, your taxation base will be the minimum. And that directly affects your future benefits: sickness leave, retirement, cessation of activity. The lower your taxation base, the lower the benefit you'll receive.
segments.visual: retirement pension calculation future planning

segments.voice: Third pitfall that almost no one mentions. If you cancel your registration as a freelancer before completing the twelve months and then re-register, you lose the flat rate. You can't resume it where you left off. You'd have to wait another two years without being registered to apply for it again. This discourages many from trying an activity for a few months and forces them to plan well.
segments.visual: stop sign warning caution red flag alert

segments.voice: There's an important exception for certain regions. Some, like certain states or counties, offer additional bonuses to the national flat rate. For example, in some areas, new freelancers under thirty or women of any age can access even lower fees or extensions of the bonus period according to regional regulations.
segments.visual: US/UK map regions flags

segments.voice: To apply for the flat rate, the process is simple. When you register with the tax authority through their online portal, you check the box for the startup bonus. You don't need to submit additional documentation at the time of registration. The system automatically checks if you meet the requirement of not having been registered in the previous two years.
segments.visual: computer screen online form registration process

segments.voice: A common mistake is to register first with the tax authority and forget to apply for the flat rate when registering with the social security system. Registration with the tax authority and the social security system are separate procedures, although they must be done within a maximum of sixty days. If you don't check the bonus when registering, it's more complicated to claim it later.
segments.visual: tax office forms bureaucracy paperwork

segments.voice: Fourth pitfall. If you're a corporate freelancer, i.e., if you register as an administrator of a Limited Company, you CAN'T access the flat rate. This bonus is exclusive to individual freelancers registered with the tax authority. Corporate freelancers have a higher minimum taxation base and don't have access to this reduction according to tax law.
segments.visual: business meeting corporate office company formation

segments.voice: Now, is the flat rate worth it? Let's do the math. Without the flat rate, a freelancer in the minimum income bracket pays around $230 per month in 2025. With the flat rate, it's $80. The difference is $150 per month, $1,800 per year. If you get the extension, it's $3,600 in two years.
segments.visual: savings calculation spreadsheet money growing chart

segments.voice: Another aspect you should know: the flat rate is compatible with the partial cessation of activity benefit. That is, if you've previously contributed enough, you can receive the cessation of activity benefit while enjoying the flat rate in a new activity. This is contemplated in tax law.
segments.visual: safety net unemployment benefits documents approval

segments.voice: Fifth pitfall. The real income taxation system requires you to forecast your net income when you register. If at the end of the year your real income is higher than forecasted, the tax authority will adjust your fee upwards, and you'll have to pay the difference. This happens the following year and can mean an unexpected payment of several hundred dollars.
segments.visual: surprised person unexpected bill payment shock

segments.voice: If your real income is lower than forecasted, the tax authority will refund the difference. But be careful, if you declared income below the minimum wage to maintain the flat rate extension and then invoice more, you'll lose the extension retroactively. The tax authority and social security system cross-check data. Don't try to cheat the system because it's more expensive.
segments.visual: data analysis cross reference government database

segments.voice: For freelancers in multiple activities, i.e., those who work as employees and also register as freelancers, there's an additional reduction of 50% in the minimum taxation base during the first eighteen months and 25% in the following eighteen months, according to tax law.
segments.visual: person working two jobs office and laptop home

segments.voice: This reduction for multiple activities is compatible with the flat rate. That is, you could pay $80 for the first twelve months and then benefit from the reduction for multiple activities during the next eighteen months. It's a combination that few advisors mention and can save you over $5,000 in three years.
segments.visual: money savings stacking coins growing investment

segments.voice: A special case is freelancers with a disability of 33% or more. They have an extended flat rate of $60 for the first twenty-four months directly, without needing to demonstrate low income for the extension. This is regulated according to tax law.
segments.visual: disability rights inclusion accessibility workplace

segments.voice: Victims of gender violence and terrorism who register as freelancers also have a special flat rate. In these cases, the reduced fee is extended to twenty-four months from the start of the activity, with the same conditions as freelancers with disabilities.
segments.visual: support protection legal rights woman empowerment

segments.voice: Sixth and final pitfall. Some managers charge between $100 and $300 to process the registration as a freelancer with a flat rate. It's a procedure you can do yourself online in less than thirty minutes through the tax authority's portal and the tax return form. Don't pay anyone for something you can do for free from your computer with your digital certificate or login credentials.
segments.visual: person using computer online government portal easy

segments.voice: Let's talk about the digital certificate. To register online, you need a digital certificate of natural person issued by the relevant authority, or the Cl@ve system. I recommend getting the digital certificate because you'll need it for everything: filing taxes, consulting the social security system, signing documents. You can request it on the authority's website and pick it up at a tax office.
segments.visual: digital certificate computer security authentication

segments.voice: In summary: the flat rate is an excellent opportunity for new freelancers, but you must plan what will happen when it ends. Save part of what you save in fees during these first months to cushion the subsequent jump. My advice is to set aside at least $100 per month from that savings for when the fee rises to its real level.
segments.visual: piggy bank savings plan financial strategy

segments.voice: To calculate your real fee after the flat rate, enter the tax authority's fee simulator on their website. Introduce your forecast of annual net income, and the system will tell you which bracket you fall into and how much you'll pay. That way, there won't be any surprises. Information is power, especially when it comes to your money.
segments.visual: simulation calculator web portal results screen

segments.voice: If this video has helped you understand the flat rate and its pitfalls, give it a like and subscribe to the channel. Each week, I publish content about personal finance and taxation for freelancers in the US/UK. Share this video with anyone thinking of becoming a freelancer; it could save them thousands of dollars and more than one headache. See you in the next video.
segments.visual: subscribe button notification bell thumbs up
